message:
feat(ci,core): add demo evidence guard and promote C4 diagrams to full support

CI pipeline:
- Add demo-evidence-guard job: runs static and React demo replay
  suites with Chromium, validates determinism summaries and replay
  bundles, uploads artifacts with 90-day retention
- Wire demo_evidence_enabled, demo_static_repeat, demo_react_repeat
  through quality-gate-config outputs
- Download demo evidence replays in evidence-ledger-guard and include
  six new artifacts (summary, manifests, replay suites) in the ledger
  bundle
- Add demo-evidence-guard to quality-summary matrix and step summary

Quality gates (.ci/quality-gates.toml):
- Add [demo_evidence] section: enabled, blocking, static_repeat=5,
  react_repeat=5

Core (fm-core):
- Promote C4 diagram family (C4Context, C4Container, C4Component,
  C4Dynamic, C4Deployment) from Partial/basic to Supported/full
- Demote XyChart from Partial to basic (standalone tier)
- Expose new cga module as public
- Update tests to match new C4 support level expectations

Layout (fm-layout):
- Minor whitespace / formatting cleanup

CLI tests:
- Adjust integration test assertions for updated support labels

README / beads:
- Update bead status tracking for demo evidence work

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-17e4.5","title":"Implement CI quality gates for alien concept adoption","description":"## CI Quality Gates for Alien Concept Adoption

Implement CI pipeline gates that enforce the graveyard methodology: no alien concept integration merges without evidence, no performance regression without justification, no golden checksum changes without approval.

## Gate Definitions

**Gate 1: Golden Checksum Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout or fm-core
- Check: golden checksums unchanged, OR commit message contains [golden-update] with justification
- Fail action: block merge, link to golden checksum update workflow

**Gate 2: Performance Regression Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout, fm-core, or fm-parser
- Check: benchmark suite runs, all metrics within 10% of baseline (configurable threshold)
- Fail action: block merge if any metric regresses > 10%, warn if > 5%
- Exception: commit message contains [perf-regression-ok: reason]

**Gate 3: Property Test Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout, fm-core, fm-parser
- Check: all proptest properties pass with 10000 cases
- Fail action: block merge on any property violation

**Gate 4: Evidence Ledger Guard**
- Trigger: any bead with label "alien-cs" moved to complete status
- Check: corresponding evidence ledger entry exists with post-metrics
- Fail action: warn (not block), link to evidence creation workflow

**Gate 5: Determinism Guard**
- Trigger: any change to fm-layout
- Check: run layout 10 times on corpus, all outputs identical
- Fail action: block merge on non-determinism

**Gate 6: Decision Contract Guard**
- Trigger: any alien-cs epic moved to complete
- Check: decision contract document exists in evidence/ directory
- Fail action: warn (not block)

## Implementation
1. Define gates as GitHub Actions workflow steps (or equivalent CI).
2. Each gate is a separate job for parallel execution.
3. Gate results aggregated into a single merge-blocking status check.
4. Override mechanism: repo admin can force-merge with audit trail.
5. Gate configuration stored in `.ci/quality-gates.toml` (thresholds, exceptions).
6. Dashboard: CI generates a quality summary comment on each PR.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 6 gates implemented and running in CI
- [ ] Gates block merge on failure (except Gate 4 and 6 which warn)
- [ ] Override mechanism exists with audit trail
- [ ] Configuration is in-repo and version-controlled
- [ ] Quality summary comment posted on each PR
- [ ] Gate execution time < 10 minutes total (parallel execution)","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:30.166665414Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T16:15:03.209640830Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["automation","ci","methodology","quality-gates"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:30.166665414Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:42.133640448Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.5","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:42.245035750Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-17e4.6","title":"Create decision contract template and initial contracts for all alien concepts","description":"## Decision Contract Template and Initial Contracts

Create a formal decision contract template for alien CS concept adoption, and draft initial contracts for all alien concepts being considered for FrankenMermaid.

## Decision Contract Template
Each alien concept adoption must have a decision contract BEFORE implementation begins. The contract specifies:

```markdown
# Decision Contract: [Concept Name] (§X.Y)

## Graveyard Score: X.X / Tier: A|B|C

## Hypothesis
[What improvement do we expect? How much? On what workloads?]

## Acceptance Criteria (Adopt)
- [ ] Metric A improved by >= X% on benchmark corpus
- [ ] No metric regressed by > Y%
- [ ] Implementation complexity <= Z LOC
- [ ] Evidence ledger entry complete with measurements

## Rejection Criteria (Reject)
- [ ] Metric A improvement < X% (threshold for "not worth it")
- [ ] Any metric regression > Y%
- [ ] Implementation complexity > Z LOC
- [ ] Solver/algorithm timeout > T ms on target workload

## Evaluation Protocol
1. Baseline measurement (pre-implementation)
2. Implementation
3. Post-measurement (same corpus, same metrics)
4. Statistical comparison with confidence intervals
5. Decision: Adopt / Reject / Defer / Hybrid

## Timeline
- Baseline: [date]
- Implementation: [date range]
- Evaluation: [date]
- Decision: [date]

## Reviewers
[Who reviews and ratifies the decision?]
```

## Initial Contracts to Draft
1. **E-Graphs for Crossing Minimization (§6.6)** — Score 3.0, Tier B
   - Accept if: >= 15% crossing reduction on dense graphs, fallback < 100ms
   - Reject if: < 5% improvement or fallback > 500ms

2. **Swiss Tables for Node/Edge Maps (§7.7)** — Score 3.0, Tier B
   - Accept if: >= 20% lookup throughput improvement, >= 5% end-to-end
   - Reject if: < 5% end-to-end improvement (already using hashbrown)

3. **Conformal Geometric Algebra (§12.11)** — Score 2.5, Tier C
   - Accept if: code clarity measurably improved, performance within 2x of matrices
   - Reject if: performance > 3x of matrices on rendering hot path

4. **Constraint Programming for Layout (§9.7)** — Score 3.5, Tier B
   - Accept if: constraints respected, solve time < 1s for 200 nodes
   - Reject if: solve time > 5s for 100 nodes

5. **Bidirectional Lenses (§6.2)** — Score 4.0, Tier B
   - Accept if: GetPut and PutGet laws hold for 100% of corpus
   - Reject if: round-trip latency > 200ms on 500-node diagram

6. **Incremental Subgraph Re-Layout (§6.1)** — Score 6.7, Tier A
   - Mandatory adoption. Accept if: < 10ms update on 1000-node diagram
   - Remediation required if: > 50ms or non-deterministic

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Decision contract template created in evidence/TEMPLATE.md
- [ ] Initial contracts drafted for all 6 alien concepts above
- [ ] Each contract has specific, measurable accept/reject criteria
- [ ] Contracts reviewed and ratified before implementation begins
- [ ] Contracts version-controlled in evidence/contracts/ directory","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:48.635623903Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:41:13.427956875Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T01:41:13.427458842Z","close_reason":"Created decision contract template (evidence/TEMPLATE.md) and 6 initial contracts: E-Graphs (§6.6), Swiss Tables (§7.7), CGA (§12.11), Constraint Programming (§9.7), Bidirectional Lenses (§6.2), Incremental Re-Layout (§6.1). Each has specific measurable accept/reject criteria, evaluation protocols, and benchmark corpus definitions.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","decision-contract","documentation","methodology"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17e4.6","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:42:48.635623903Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

-{"id":"bd-1a45","title":"C4: Implement technology labels, element icons, and legend rendering","description":"# C4 Diagram Full Rendering

## What mermaid-js Supports
C4 diagrams have rich element types with icons, descriptions, and technology labels:
- Person: stick figure icon
- System/Container/Component: rectangles with type label, name, technology, description
- External systems: dashed borders
- Databases: cylinder shape
- Queues: parallelogram shape
- Boundaries: labeled dashed rectangles
- Relationships: arrows with labels and technology annotations
- Legend: auto-generated showing element type → visual mapping
- LAYOUT_TOP_DOWN() / LAYOUT_LEFT_RIGHT() directives

## Current State
- All element types ARE parsed
- All relationship types ARE parsed
- Boundaries ARE parsed
- Technology/description fields within function args are NOT fully extracted (only first arg parsed)
- Element type icons NOT rendered
- Legend NOT rendered
- Layout directives parsed but NOT applied

## Implementation Plan
1. **Parser**: Parse all positional arguments in C4 function calls: (id, name, [technology], [description]). Store technology and description on IrNode.
2. **SVG Renderer**: Render element type labels (<<Person>>, <<System>>, etc.). Show technology in brackets below name. Show description in smaller text. Render person as stick figure. Render databases as cylinders. Render external elements with dashed borders.
3. **Layout**: Honor LAYOUT_TOP_DOWN/LEFT_RIGHT by setting graph direction.
4. **Legend**: Generate a legend box showing element type mapping.
5. **Tests**: Full C4 context diagram, container diagram, component diagram with all element types.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-17T02:47:12.054865942Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T18:44:17.432538691Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["c4","parity","parser","render"],"comments":[{"id":169,"issue_id":"bd-1a45","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Testing Contract (Mandatory)

### Integration Tests

- Determinism check: same input -> identical output (3 runs)
- Graceful degradation: partially valid input produces best-effort output with diagnostics

### Quality Gates

- cargo check --workspace --all-targets

- cargo test --workspace (zero new failures)

 {"id":"bd-2q3f","title":"EPIC: Geometric/Conformal Algebra for Transforms (§12.11)","description":"## Geometric/Conformal Algebra for Transforms (§12.11)

Replace the conventional matrix-based coordinate transform pipeline (translation, rotation, scaling, reflection) with Conformal Geometric Algebra (CGA), unifying all rigid-body and conformal transformations under a single algebraic framework. This enables elegant composition of transforms, intersection queries, and constraint solving using the same multivector operations.

## Motivation
FrankenMermaid's rendering pipeline applies many coordinate transforms: viewport mapping, zoom/pan, node placement, edge routing with Bezier curves, text positioning, and SVG viewBox calculations. Currently these use a mix of 2D affine matrices and ad-hoc vector arithmetic. CGA unifies ALL of these into a single algebra where:
- Points, lines, circles, and planes are all represented as multivectors
- Translation, rotation, and scaling are all rotors (geometric products of vectors)
- Intersection of any two geometric objects is a single algebraic operation (meet/join)
- Transforms compose by rotor multiplication (associative, no matrix decomposition needed)

## Mathematical Foundation
Conformal Geometric Algebra embeds 2D Euclidean space into a 4D projective space via the conformal model R_{3,1}:
- Basis vectors: e1, e2 (Euclidean), e+ (positive extra), e- (negative extra)
- Null vectors: e_o = (e- - e+)/2 (origin), e_inf = e+ + e- (infinity)
- Point embedding: P(x,y) = x*e1 + y*e2 + (x^2+y^2)/2 * e_inf + e_o
- Translation rotor: T = 1 - (d/2)*e_inf where d is displacement bivector
- Rotation rotor: R = cos(t/2) + sin(t/2)*e12 where e12 = e1 wedge e2
- Scaling rotor: S = cosh(s/2) + sinh(s/2)*e+ wedge e-
- Composed transform: M = T*R*S (just multiply rotors)
- Apply to point: P' = M*P*M_rev (sandwich product, M_rev is reverse of M)

## Key Papers
- Dorst, Fontijne & Mann, "Geometric Algebra for Computer Science" (2007)
- Hildenbrand, "Foundations of Geometric Algebra Computing" (2013)
- Wareham, Cameron & Lasenby, "Applications of CGA" (2005)
- De Keninck & Dorst, "Guided Tour of GA" (2019)

## Graveyard Reference: §12.11 -- Geometric/Conformal Algebra for Transforms
Score: 2.5 (Alien uplift). Risk: learning curve, performance vs optimized matrix code.

## Success Metrics
- All coordinate transforms in rendering pipeline expressed as CGA rotors
- Transform composition 3-5 operations instead of matrix multiply (code clarity metric)
- Intersection queries (edge-node, viewport clipping) expressed as meet/join operations

-{"id":"bd-2q3f.1","title":"Implement CGA multivector types and rotor algebra in fm-core","description":"## CGA Multivector Types and Rotor Algebra (§12.11.1)

Implement the foundational Conformal Geometric Algebra types: multivectors in R_{3,1}, rotor construction, sandwich product, and conversion to/from conventional 2D affine matrices.

## Mathematical Foundation
In the conformal model R_{3,1} for 2D geometry:
- **Basis:** {1, e1, e2, e+, e-, e12, e1+, e1-, e2+, e2-, e+-, e12+, e12-, e1+-, e2+-, e12+-}
  That is 2^4 = 16 basis blades for a general multivector.
- **Even subalgebra (rotors):** {1, e12, e1+, e1-, e2+, e2-, e+-, e12+-} — 8 components
  Rotors are even-grade multivectors with unit norm: R*R̃ = 1.
- **Key operations:**
  - Geometric product: ab = a·b + a∧b (combines inner and outer product)
  - Sandwich product: x' = RxR̃ (applies transform R to object x)
  - Reverse: R̃ reverses the order of basis vectors in each blade
  - Norm: |R|² = R*R̃ (scalar part)

**Rotor construction helpers:**
- `Rotor::translation(dx, dy)`: T = 1 - (dx*e1 + dy*e2)*e_∞/2
- `Rotor::rotation(angle)`: R = cos(θ/2) + sin(θ/2)*e1∧e2
- `Rotor::scale(factor)`: S = cosh(ln(s)/2) + sinh(ln(s)/2)*e+∧e-
- `Rotor::compose(r1, r2)`: M = r1 * r2 (geometric product)

**Conversion to affine matrix:**
Extract the 2x3 affine matrix [a,b,tx; c,d,ty] from a CGA rotor by applying the rotor to the basis points (0,0), (1,0), (0,1) and reading off coefficients. This is the bridge to conventional rendering.

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Create `fm-core/src/cga.rs` module with `Multivector4D` type (16 f64 components, stored as [f64; 16]).
2. Implement `Rotor` type (wrapper over even-grade multivector, 8 f64 components).
3. Implement geometric product, sandwich product, reverse, norm.
4. Implement rotor constructors: translation, rotation, scale, reflection.
5. Implement `Rotor::to_affine_matrix() -> AffineMatrix2D` conversion.
6. Implement `AffineMatrix2D::to_rotor() -> Rotor` conversion (for interop).
7. SIMD optimization: use `std::simd` or `packed_simd` for the 8-component rotor multiply.
8. Comprehensive property tests: rotor composition associativity, inverse correctness, matrix round-trip.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Multivector and Rotor types with full geometric product implementation
- [ ] All rotor constructors produce correct transforms (verified against matrix equivalents)
- [ ] Sandwich product correctly transforms points, lines, circles
- [ ] Rotor↔AffineMatrix round-trip error < 1e-12 (property test, 10000 random rotors)
- [ ] Rotor composition is associative to within 1e-10 (property test)
- [ ] SIMD-optimized rotor multiply benchmarked against scalar baseline
- [ ] #[no_std] compatible for WASM target","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:37:54.293509980Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:37:54.293509980Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","conformal","geometric-algebra","math"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2q3f.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2q3f","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:37:54.293509980Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-2q3f.2","title":"Replace rendering transform pipeline with CGA rotor composition","description":"## CGA Rotor Composition in Rendering Pipeline (§12.11.2)

Replace the current matrix-based transform stack in fm-render-svg and fm-render-term with CGA rotor composition, enabling elegant multi-stage transform pipelines.

## Mathematical Foundation
Current pipeline: SVG rendering applies transforms as matrix multiplications:
  M_final = M_viewport * M_zoom * M_pan * M_node_placement
Each M is a 3x3 homogeneous matrix (or 2x3 affine). Transform composition: 6 multiplies per matrix pair, 18 multiply-adds for 3x3.

CGA pipeline: same transforms as rotor composition:
  R_final = R_viewport * R_zoom * R_pan * R_node_placement
Each R is an 8-component even multivector. Composition: geometric product of even multivectors, ~64 multiply-adds. BUT: rotors compose more naturally (no matrix decomposition needed for extracting rotation/scale/translation), and the composed rotor can be cached and applied to many points via sandwich product.

**Key advantage:** In CGA, extracting "what rotation is embedded in this composed transform" is trivial (read off the e12 component). In matrices, this requires SVD or atan2 decomposition. This matters for text rendering (text must remain upright even when diagram is rotated).

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Define `TransformStack` type that stores a composed CGA rotor instead of a matrix stack.
2. Push/pop operations compose/decompose rotors.
3. For SVG output: convert final rotor to `transform="matrix(a,b,c,d,e,f)"` attribute.
4. For terminal output: convert final rotor to integer cell coordinates (with rounding).
5. For text rendering: extract rotation component from rotor to counter-rotate text.
6. Implement viewport clipping using CGA meet operation (line ∧ circle = intersection points).
7. Cache composed rotors per diagram region for incremental re-rendering.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TransformStack API used in both SVG and terminal renderers
- [ ] SVG output bit-identical to current matrix-based output (regression test)
- [ ] Text counter-rotation works correctly for rotated diagrams
- [ ] Viewport clipping via CGA meet operation produces correct clip paths
- [ ] Benchmark: rotor pipeline within 2x of matrix pipeline for 10K-node rendering
- [ ] Transform stack push/pop is O(1) (rotor multiply)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:38:09.355229963Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.337378188Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","conformal","geometric-algebra","rendering"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2q3f.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2q3f","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:38:09.355229963Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2q3f.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2q3f.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.337319388Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2q3f.3","title":"Implement CGA intersection queries for edge routing and hit testing","description":"## CGA Intersection Queries for Edge Routing and Hit Testing (§12.11.3)

Use Conformal Geometric Algebra's meet and join operations to implement intersection queries needed by edge routing (edge-node intersection, edge-edge crossing detection) and interactive hit testing (point-in-node, cursor-to-edge distance).

## Mathematical Foundation
In CGA, geometric objects are represented as blades (multivector components):
- **Point:** P = x*e1 + y*e2 + (x²+y²)/2*e_∞ + e_o (grade-1 null vector)
- **Point pair / Line:** L = P1 ∧ P2 (outer product of two points, grade-2 blade)
- **Circle:** C = P1 ∧ P2 ∧ P3 (outer product of three points, grade-3 blade)
- **Plane/Line (dual):** π = C* (dual of the circle, grade-1 blade)

**Intersection (meet):** The regressive product (meet) of two objects gives their intersection:
- Line ∧ Line → Point (or empty if parallel)
- Line ∧ Circle → PointPair (0, 1, or 2 intersection points)
- Circle ∧ Circle → PointPair (0, 1, or 2 intersection points)

**Distance:** The inner product of two null vectors gives the squared distance:
  d²(P, Q) = -2 * P·Q

**Point-in-region:** A point P is inside circle C iff P·C > 0 (positive inner product).

These operations replace ad-hoc geometric computations scattered throughout the codebase:
- Edge-node intersection: line_segment.meet(node_boundary_circle)
- Edge-edge crossing: line1.meet(line2), check if intersection point is within both segments
- Hit testing: point.inner(node_boundary), sign determines inside/outside

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Implement `CgaPoint`, `CgaLine`, `CgaCircle` wrapper types over multivectors.
2. Implement `meet()` operation for all pairs.
3. Implement `distance()` and `contains()` using inner product.
4. Replace edge-node intersection code in fm-layout edge routing with CGA meet.
5. Replace hit testing in fm-render (WASM interactive mode) with CGA contains.
6. Handle degenerate cases: parallel lines (no intersection), tangent circles (single point).
7. For rectangular nodes: approximate bounding box as 4 line segments, or use exact CGA representation of line pairs.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Meet operation correct for line-line, line-circle, circle-circle (verified against analytic solutions)
- [ ] Edge routing uses CGA intersection for node boundary clipping
- [ ] Hit testing uses CGA point-in-region for interactive mode
- [ ] Degenerate cases handled without NaN/panic (property test with random geometry)
- [ ] Performance: CGA intersection within 3x of specialized analytic code
- [ ] All edge routing visual tests produce identical output to baseline","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:38:26.836452381Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.450135660Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","conformal","edge-routing","geometric-algebra"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2q3f.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2q3f","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:38:26.836452381Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2q3f.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2q3f.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.450040953Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2q3f.4","title":"Benchmark CGA vs matrix transforms and publish decision contract","description":"## Benchmark CGA vs Matrix Transforms and Decision Contract (§12.11.4)

Rigorous performance comparison of CGA-based transforms against optimized 2D affine matrix transforms, producing a decision contract that specifies when CGA is justified vs when to fall back to matrices.

## Mathematical Foundation
**Operation complexity comparison:**

| Operation | Matrix (3x3) | CGA Rotor (8-component) |
|---|---|---|
| Compose two transforms | 27 mul + 18 add | ~64 mul + ~48 add |
| Apply to one point | 6 mul + 4 add | ~48 mul + ~32 add |
| Apply to N points (batched) | 6N mul + 4N add | ~48N mul + ~32N add (or extract matrix: 6N+48) |
| Extract rotation angle | atan2(m01, m00) — 1 trig | read e12 component — 0 trig |
| Extract translation | read m02, m12 | 4 mul + 2 add |
| Intersect two lines | 6 mul + ~10 branch | ~32 mul, no branch |

**Break-even analysis:** For applying a composed transform to N points:
- Matrix: 6N operations (after composing once for 27 ops)
- CGA: 48N operations (after composing once for 64 ops), OR extract matrix (48 ops) then 6N
- Strategy: compose with CGA, extract matrix, apply matrix for N > 1.

## Implementation in FrankenMermaid
1. Create `benches/transforms.rs` with criterion.rs.
2. Benchmark scenarios:
   a. Single transform application (1 point)
   b. Composed transform application (4 chained transforms, 1 point)
   c. Batch transform (1 composed transform, N points for N in {10, 100, 1000, 10000})
   d. Intersection queries (line-line, line-circle, 1000 queries)
   e. Transform decomposition (extract rotation, translation, scale)
3. Measure: throughput (ops/sec), latency (ns/op), cache misses (perf counters if available).
4. Test on both x86_64 (with AVX2) and aarch64 (with NEON) targets.
5. Test WASM target (no SIMD vs SIMD proposal).

## Decision Contract Output
The benchmark produces a decision document specifying:
- **Use CGA for:** transform composition, constraint solving, intersection queries, rotation extraction
- **Use matrix for:** batch point transformation (N > threshold), SVG attribute generation
- **Hybrid strategy:** CGA compose → extract matrix → batch apply (best of both worlds)
- **Threshold N:** the point count above which matrix extraction is faster than direct CGA application
- **If CGA overhead > 3x for any critical path:** document and use matrix with CGA API wrapper

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Criterion benchmarks for all 5 scenario categories
- [ ] Results on x86_64, aarch64, and wasm32 targets
- [ ] Decision contract document with specific thresholds and recommendations
- [ ] Hybrid strategy (CGA compose + matrix apply) benchmarked and proven optimal
- [ ] Results reproducible with fixed seed and deterministic inputs
- [ ] Decision contract reviewed and ratified (evidence-based, not opinion-based)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:38:45.943517322Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:04.684579288Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-cs","benchmark","decision-contract","geometric-algebra"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2q3f.4","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:00.350604580Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2q3f.4","depends_on_id":"bd-17e4.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:30:04.684459053Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2q3f.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2q3f","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:38:45.943517322Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2q3f.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2q3f.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.569194814Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2q3f.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2q3f.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:47:41.686351295Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
